title: IAM policy grants Action: "*" on Resource: "*" — full AWS account access
exploit: Any principal assuming app-role has unrestricted access to every AWS service and resource in the account — S3 delete, EC2 terminate, IAM create-user, etc.
fix: Replace with least-privilege policy scoped to the specific actions and resources the app needs

title: S3 bucket has public-read ACL — all objects world-readable
exploit: Anyone on the internet can enumerate and download every object in the bucket; no auth required
fix: Remove acl = "public-read"; use aws_s3_bucket_public_access_block to enforce private

title: RDS instance not encrypted at rest (storage_encrypted = false)
exploit: DB volume snapshot or direct storage access exposes all table data in plaintext
fix: Set storage_encrypted = true and specify kms_key_id for customer-managed KMS key

title: RDS instance publicly accessible from the internet
exploit: DB port 3306 reachable from any IP (compounded by sg-unrestricted-db-port); brute-force or CVE exploitation possible without VPC boundary
fix: Set publicly_accessible = false; restrict ingress to application subnet CIDR only

title: Security group allows SSH (port 22) from 0.0.0.0/0
exploit: Any host on the internet can attempt SSH brute-force or exploit OpenSSH CVEs against any EC2 instance in this SG
fix: Restrict cidr_blocks to known admin IP ranges or use AWS SSM Session Manager and remove port 22 entirely

title: Plaintext DB password committed to terraform.tfvars in version control
exploit: Anyone with repo read access obtains the production DB master password; git history preserves it even after deletion
fix: Remove terraform.tfvars from version control; use AWS Secrets Manager or HashiCorp Vault; reference via data source

title: Security group allows MySQL (port 3306) from 0.0.0.0/0
exploit: DB port directly reachable from the internet; compounded by rds-publicly-accessible; exposes MySQL to brute-force and protocol exploits
fix: Remove the 3306 ingress rule; access DB only from the application subnet or via a bastion

title: S3 bucket has no server-side encryption configuration
exploit: Objects stored without SSE; AWS S3 now enables SSE-S3 by default but explicit config is required for KMS-managed keys and audit compliance
fix: Add aws_s3_bucket_server_side_encryption_configuration with AES256 or aws:kms algorithm
